articles.title: Agglomeration, Segregation and Imperial Origins -- by Ester Faia, Edward L. Glaeser, Saverio Simonelli, Martina Viarengo
articles.author: National Bureau of Economic Research Working Papers
articles.summary: What explains the dramatic differences in earnings across locations? We employ an administrative employer-employee linked dataset from Italy that includes the country’s entire workforce to estimate firm-worker or location-worker effects. We also estimate differences in human capital accumulation across firms and cities. We find that the elasticity of the location premia to density is smaller than in other settings and that other locational characteristics, such as segregation in school or the workplace and inter-generational mobility, are more strongly correlated with earnings and earnings growth. Our place-based estimates are similar if we focus on movers who were forced to relocate after the L’Aquila Earthquake. Using a regression discontinuity design, we find that density levels jump up at the historic border between House of Savoy-ruled Piedmont and the Hapsburg Empire. Earnings today also jump at the border. This finding suggests that there may be some unintended effects of being a far-flung province of a distant empire, perhaps because of access to larger markets or the administrative and educational reforms that began under Empress Maria Theresa.
articles.reason: Aglomeración urbana y diferencias salariales territoriales

articles.title: Understanding the Employment Effects of Opportunity Zones -- by Matthew Freedman, Noah Arman Kouchekinia, David Neumark
articles.author: National Bureau of Economic Research Working Papers
articles.summary: The Opportunity Zone program was designed to encourage investment in distressed communities across the United States. Early research found no evidence of impacts of the program on employment, earnings, or poverty of zone residents, but some evidence of positive effects on employment among businesses in zones. Using the latest survey-based as well as administrative data, we adopt a longer-run and more comprehensive perspective on the labor market impacts of OZs. We find that OZ designation increases job creation among businesses within zones. However, a large share of the newly created jobs in zones is offset by declines in nearby low-income communities. While we detect gains in OZ resident employment over the longer run, the increase comes from jobs with workplaces outside of OZs that, in light of the changing demographic composition of zones, are likely held by new as opposed to existing residents. Overall, our results suggest that OZs have limited benefits for existing residents of targeted areas and are associated mainly with a spatial reallocation of jobs and households.

articles.summary: The world started pulling money out of America when Trump's aggressive actions made investors worried. This caused U.S. bond yields to rise and the dollar to fall, signaling capital flight. If this continues, it could hurt the U.S. economy and living standards badly.

articles.title: Causation Does not Imply Variation
articles.author: John H. Cochrane
articles.summary: Causal identification shows whether x can move y, but it often uses tiny, special slices of data. Those identified effects usually explain only a small share of the overall variation in outcomes. We still need broader approaches to understand the main causes of big economic facts.

articles.title: The UK goes nuclear
articles.author: Alex Chalmers
articles.summary:
The UK wants to cut nuclear costs and speed building after becoming the world’s most expensive place to build plants.  
A taskforce recommends a single lead regulator, clearer risk thresholds, fewer needless environmental fixes, and limits on legal delays.  
If the government fully adopts these changes, nuclear could become affordable and deliverable again.

articles.title: Group differences in voting, 2016-2024
articles.author: David Weakliem (noreply@blogger.com)
articles.summary: This post updates voting trends from 2016 to 2024, showing how different groups voted. Trump gained support among Latino and Asian voters, while his support among Black voters increased slightly. Overall, there were small changes in voting patterns, with Trump making gains across various demographics.

articles.title: ¿Es rentable invertir en educación pública?
articles.author: admin
articles.summary:
Un estudio para 38 países europeos (2002–2020) mide el retorno social del gasto público en educación usando bienestar subjetivo.  
El gasto educativo aumenta la satisfacción con la vida, pero solo la educación terciaria tiene un efecto positivo y consistente.  
El gasto en primaria y secundaria no muestra efecto significativo, lo que plantea dudas sobre la asignación de recursos y sugiere estudiar otros contextos.

articles.title: Donald Trump and Joe McCarthy
articles.author: Andrew
articles.summary: Richard Rovere’s book about Senator Joe McCarthy shows many similarities between McCarthy and Donald Trump, such as their skill in manipulating public opinion and their bold lies. Both drew large followings by attacking respected figures and ignoring reality. However, Trump has more direct control over government, while McCarthy relied on indirect influence.

articles.title: El fuerte aumento del gasto para mantenimiento de la red de alta velocidad va por detrás del boom de viajeros
articles.author: Raúl Sánchez
articles.summary: The number of high-speed train passengers in Spain has grown nearly 70%, but maintenance spending has only increased by 38%. Despite more travelers, the public company Adif AV faces financial losses while investing heavily in infrastructure.

articles.title: The Long Run Economic Effects of Medical Innovation and the Role of Opportunities -- by Sonia R. Bhalotra, Damian Clarke, Atheendar Venkataramani
articles.author: National Bureau of Economic Research Working Papers
articles.summary: We leverage the introduction of the first antibiotic therapies in 1937 to examine the long-run effects of early-childhood pneumonia on adult educational attainment, employment, income, and work-related disability. Using census data, we document large average improvements across all outcomes, alongside substantial heterogeneity by gender and race. Among women, health gains led to changes in marriage and fertility that partially offset their labor market improvements. Among Black Americans, we uncover a pronounced gradient linked to systemic racial discrimination in the pre–Civil Rights era: individuals born in more discriminatory Jim Crow states realized much smaller gains than those born in less discriminatory states, despite larger reductions in pneumonia exposure. There is no similar gradient among white Americans. Together, these findings highlight the central role of institutional environments in shaping whether investments in early-life health translate into long-run socioeconomic gains.

articles.title: All together now?

Democrats lost more ground in larger states, but overall, the changes in voting patterns were consistent across elections.

articles.title: Age of Invention: How Coal Really Won
articles.author: Anton Howes

The shift to burning coal in homes was influenced by fuel-saving inventions from Germany, particularly for brewing industries. By the late 1570s, demand for coal surged, leading to a significant increase in its consumption in London homes.

articles.title: Against Against Boomers
articles.author: Scott Alexander
articles.summary:
People are loudly blaming Boomers for many problems, but the data show Boomers largely improved prosperity and passed on better lives.  
Generational differences in politics and behavior are smaller than the rhetoric claims, and many complaints boil down to normal wealth and demographic shifts.  
Turning everything into identity politics about age risks repeating unfair, harmful patterns we’ll someday inflict on younger generations.

articles.title: Public opinion on immigration:  the Trump and Biden years
articles.author: David Weakliem (noreply@blogger.com)
articles.summary: Public opinion on immigration has shifted over the years, moving from a preference for "decreased" immigration in the mid-1990s to a more recent preference for "increased" immigration by 2020. However, during Biden's presidency, both Democrats and Republicans have started to favor decreasing immigration again. This change suggests that people's views may be influenced by media coverage rather than personal experience.

articles.title: Starmer's stability promise

articles.author: Finance & economics

articles.title: The Democratic base isn’t in the mood to compromise

articles.title: Some thoughts on empirical distributions of z-scores
articles.author: Andrew
articles.summary: Z-scores are estimated effects divided by their standard errors and, for each study, follow a normal sampling distribution centered on the true signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) with SD 1. Published z-score distributions are distorted by selective reporting, which hides many results near zero and biases what we observe. Understanding the true SNR distribution and selection process is key to correcting biases and avoiding overestimates.

articles.title: Where does a liberal go from here?

articles.title: How much of “Mississippi’s education miracle” is an artifact of selection bias?
articles.author: Andrew
articles.summary: The claim that Mississippi achieved an “education miracle” may be exaggerated because of selection bias. Changes in which students take tests or stay in school can make scores look better without real learning gains. Careful analysis is needed to separate true improvement from artifacts of who is measured.

articles.title: Sociology of science: What does it take for erroneous or fraudulent claims to take hold?
articles.author: Andrew
articles.summary:
Bad or fraudulent scientific claims persist because a small core of compromised researchers create misleading evidence and a larger group of influencers actively promote and defend it.  
This propagation often involves suppression of dissent, favorable publicity, and institutional support rather than mere accidental error.  
As a result, bad ideas can be kept alive despite failed replications and clear problems.

articles.title: Patterns in University Applications: Socioeconomic Status, Gender, and Subject vs. Institution Preferences
articles.author: IZA Discussion Papers, Institute of Labor Economics (IZA) | IDEAS/RePEc
articles.summary: Downloadable! This paper examines university application patterns in the UK, focusing on the joint decision of selecting both an institution and a subject. Using administrative data from the Universities and Colleges Admissions Service (UCAS) covering almost all undergraduate applications between 2008 and 2021, we document three key facts: (i) students generally choose subject before university: they apply on average to around 1.6 subject areas across 4.6 institutions, and roughly half apply to a single field across multiple universities; (ii) there are significant gender gaps in application and offer rates that reflect field composition; (iii) high-socioeconomic status students submit more applications, apply less to local institutions, and obtain more offers, but these differences shrink sharply once we control for attainment and the selectivity of the programmes that students apply to. articles.title: Women's Power in the Household -- by Seema Jayachandran, Alessandra Voena
articles.author: National Bureau of Economic Research Working Papers
articles.summary: We examine women's household power in low- and middle-income countries, synthesizing theoretical frameworks and empirical evidence on its measurement, determinants, and consequences. We define women's household power as their influence over household choices, distinguishing it from broader empowerment concepts. We review economic models, including unitary, collective, and bargaining frameworks, and map these to empirical approaches. We then discuss measurement methods  such as structural estimation of consumption allocation, survey measures, and laboratory experiments. On the determinants of women's power, we find that some approaches, such as transfers targeted to women, show mixed results, while others, such as increasing women's control over their earnings, show clearer positive impacts. On the effects of women's power, we pay special attention to children's human capital. Few studies provide strong evidence that mothers invest more in children than fathers do, but collectively the evidence suggests such an effect. We conclude by highlighting research and methodological gaps.

articles.author: chris
articles.summary: Politicians prefer lazy moralizing to intellectual enquiry. I say this because of Sunak's speech last week in which he condemned far-right extremists "who are hostile to our values and have no respect for our democratic traditions." What he did not...

articles.title: Generous Long-Term Contracts -- by Sylvain Chassang
articles.author: National Bureau of Economic Research Working Papers
articles.summary: This paper argues that in long-term consumer–producer relationships, menus of contracts can often be advantageously replaced by a single generous contract such that, at any point in time, a consumer’s cumulative transfers equal the cumulative transfers they would have made under the contract that would have been best for them in hindsight. Such generous long-term contracts can increase skeptical consumers’ demand for complex and higher-powered contracts while approximately implementing the same outcomes as the underlying menu evaluated by a rational decision maker. Applications include voluntary load shedding in retail electricity markets and cost sharing in health insurance.

articles.title: Redes y capital social después de una guerra.
articles.author: admin
articles.summary: War causes great human loss and damages social trust between groups. However, in some cases, shared trauma can strengthen community bonds and increase social participation, as shown by the lasting effects of the 1937 bombing of the Málaga-Almería road in Spain. This study finds that despite the tragedy, affected towns showed stronger social cohesion and higher voter turnout for decades after the event.

articles.title: Which way is up?
articles.author: David Weakliem (noreply@blogger.com)
articles.summary: A recent New York Times discussion highlighted that more registered voters believe America is on the right track, with 44% supporting this view. However, this sentiment does not strongly correlate with presidential approval ratings, as seen with Trump and Obama. The rising "right direction" numbers may reflect how well a president can implement their agenda, influencing approval and perceptions of the country’s conditions.

articles.title: On classical music
articles.author: chris
articles.summary: Classical music is in crisis. Those who should be promoting it seem embarrassed to do so; some universities are closing music departments; and Alexandra Wilson and Ian Pace fear that it is being sidelined because of concerns about elitism and...

articles.title: A slew of improvements to NUTS
articles.author: Bob Carpenter
articles.summary: The NUTS sampler has many new improvements that work well together to make sampling faster and more reliable. These include better mass matrix adaptation, smarter step size control, and new ways to monitor convergence quickly. Future ideas focus on making NUTS work better on GPUs and adapting locally for even more efficiency.

articles.title: Welcome to Chaos World
articles.author: Noah Smith
articles.summary: Maduro’s capture by Trump’s forces has thrown global order into chaos. The raid showed U.S. power acting unpredictably and for unilateral goals, not to uphold international norms. This unpredictability weakens deterrence and makes the world more lawless.

articles.title: Of course motherhood drives the gender wage gap
articles.author: Ruxandra Teslo
articles.summary: Motherhood causes a big part of the gender wage gap because women take time off and work less after having children. New studies using IVF data tried to challenge this but had flaws and mixed results. A recent natural experiment with women who cannot have children proves that motherhood strongly affects women's earnings and career paths.

articles.title: Identity and Ideology in the School Boardroom -- by Barbara Biasi, Minseon Park, John D. Singleton, Seth D. Zimmerman
articles.author: National Bureau of Economic Research Working Papers
articles.summary: School boards have statutory authority over most elementary and secondary education policies, but receive little attention compared to other actors in education systems. A fundamental challenge to understanding the importance of boards is the absence of data on the policy goals of board members—i.e., their ideologies—forcing researchers to conduct tests based on demographic and professional characteristics—i.e., identities—with which ideology is presumed to correlate. This paper uses new data on the viewpoints and policy actions of school board members, coupled with a regression discontinuity design that generates quasi-random variation in board composition, to establish two results. The first is that the priorities of board members have large causal effects across many domains. For example, the effect of electing an equity-focused board member on test scores for low-income students is roughly equivalent to assigning every such student a teacher who is 0.3 to 0.4 SDs higher in the distribution of teacher value-added. The second is that observing policy priorities is crucial. Identity turns out to be a poor proxy for ideology, with limited governance effects that are fully explained by differences in policy priorities. Our findings challenge the belief that school boards are unimportant, showing that who serves on the board and what they prioritize can have far-reaching consequences for students.

articles.title: Raila Amolo Odinga

articles.summary: The man whose political career defined Kenya’s multiparty politics

articles.title: Where the ducks are?
articles.author: David Weakliem (noreply@blogger.com)
articles.summary: Since the 2024 election, some believe the Democrats should move towards the center to win more votes. However, the shift in party ideology and educational levels shows that many voters may simply be adopting new labels instead of changing their beliefs. Additionally, while Democrats have become more liberal, independents are becoming more moderate, suggesting there are more potential moderate voters available.

articles.title: Aftermath
articles.author: David Weakliem (noreply@blogger.com)
articles.summary: Trump won in 2024 because voters expected a normal Republican term but reacted against his radical second term. Immigration concerns helped Trump more than inflation in the election. The Republican Party let Trump run again to avoid division, hoping his influence would fade after leaving office.

articles.title: The cathedral, the bazaar, and statistical workflow
articles.author: Andrew
articles.summary: The essay compares two styles of software development: the careful, planned "cathedral" and the open, chaotic "bazaar." The author says good work combines both styles, using R and Stan as examples of each extreme. In science and statistics, building trust requires a balanced workflow like a well-designed cathedral and bazaar working together.
articles.reason: Flujo trabajo estadístico catedral vs bazar

articles.title: The Impact of Preschool Entry Age on Children’s Behavioral and Developmental Health in Medicaid -- by Maya Rossin-Slater, Adrienne Sabety, Aileen Wu
articles.author: National Bureau of Economic Research Working Papers
articles.summary: Preschools help identify and treat learning problems early. Children born just before the school cutoff are more likely to be diagnosed with attention, speech, or hearing issues. Early preschool exposure helps explain why some kids get diagnosed sooner than others.

articles.title: Book Review (12/25): The Second Emancipation
articles.author: Ken Opalo
articles.summary: Explorations in African leadership

articles.title: Survey Statistics: weights and MRP for voters
articles.author: shira
articles.summary: Survey methods like inverse-response-probability weights and multilevel regression with poststratification (MRP) help estimate population averages. For subgroups like voters, we must adjust using turnout probabilities and voting behavior models, since voter status is unknown and biased in surveys. These approaches combine weighting and modeling to reduce bias and improve estimates for likely voters.

articles.title: The Friendship Recession:  men, women, both, or neither?
articles.author: David Weakliem (noreply@blogger.com)
articles.summary: Surveys show fewer close friends for both men and women now than in 1990, but the drop is similar for both. It's unclear if this change reflects real social shifts or differences in how surveys were done. Overall, we don't have enough data to say if friendships are truly declining or if men and women differ much in their social connections.

articles.title: Left, right, and elite, part 2
articles.author: David Weakliem (noreply@blogger.com)
articles.summary: Elites and the public have different views on foreign policy priorities, and these differences do not simply follow left or right political lines. Elites tend to care less about protecting jobs and immigration control, but more about military power and defending allies. These differences come from elites' knowledge, experience, and unique ideas about how the world works.

articles.title: A vignette study of work from home and attitudes to household work
articles.author: VoxEU | CEPR
articles.summary: Working from home (WFH) changes how people expect household chores and childcare to be shared. When a man works from home, people expect him to do more housework than when a woman works from home. This shift could help gender equality at home but may increase inequality between different types of workers.

articles.title: Big Questions, Big Data!
articles.author: Alice Evans
articles.summary:
Migration weakens caste controls and lets Indians pursue work, mixed friendships, and upward mobility through education.  
Saudi funding of Wahhabism and religious schools helped spread stricter orthodoxies and ostracize other Muslims.  
The author calls for big data — social networks, surnames, TV scripts and school enrollments — to measure cultural change and the gender gap globally.

articles.title: Survey Statistics: quantity vs quality
articles.author: shira
articles.summary: Surveys face trade-offs between quantity (coverage and sample size) and quality (how response depends on the outcome). A large dataset can still be biased if response varies with the variable of interest, while a small, well-behaved sample can be less biased. Analysts can try combining surveys in one model, but understanding each survey’s errors may be wiser.

articles.title: Friendship recession, part 4
articles.author: David Weakliem (noreply@blogger.com)
articles.summary: More young men report having no close friends now compared to 1990, but survey methods may affect these numbers. Overall, the average number of close friends has declined slightly for both men and women. The idea of friendship may be changing, focusing more on discussing personal problems than just having friends.

articles.title: Left, right, and elite
articles.author: David Weakliem (noreply@blogger.com)
articles.summary: Political elites often have different views from the general public, especially on issues like assisted dying. Despite strong public support, elites hesitate to change laws due to concerns about complex rules and consequences. This shows that elites are a small, distinct group with unique perspectives beyond simple left-right politics.

articles.title: The Politics of Public Service Reform
articles.author: IZA Discussion Papers, Institute of Labor Economics (IZA) | IDEAS/RePEc
articles.summary: A big education reform improved school quality in a developing country. However, it lowered support for the ruling party and made teachers less happy and less involved in politics. The reform lost the most votes where teachers became less politically engaged.

articles.title: Some changes to Sustainability by Numbers in the new year
articles.author: Hannah Ritchie
articles.summary: Hannah Ritchie will broaden her Substack in 2026 to cover not just environmental topics but also demographics, global health, technology, and development. She will keep a data-led approach and rename the newsletter “By the Numbers.” She expects some subscribers may leave but hopes readers will stay as she explores these wider issues.

articles.title: The signal-to-noise ratio in statistics
articles.author: Erik van Zwet
articles.summary: When an unbiased estimator y has known standard error s, the z-statistic z = y/s is normally distributed with mean SNR = theta/s and SD 1. The signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) shows how large the true effect is compared with estimation noise and thus how informative an experiment is. Histograms of z or p-values from many studies reveal a lack of small |z| (and a drop near p=0.05), suggesting selective reporting or other distortions.

articles.title: When after all it was you, not me
articles.author: David Weakliem (noreply@blogger.com)
articles.summary: Some surveys show worrying support for political violence, but their methods and results are often unreliable or hard to interpret. People’s views on political violence can depend on who the victim is and their party loyalty. Political leaders’ harsh language may make these feelings worse, especially among Republicans.

articles.title: "When will I get credit....?  When will people understand.....?  When will Polls reflect the Greatness of America...?"
articles.author: David Weakliem (noreply@blogger.com)
articles.summary: People’s views on the economy often depend on their political party and how much they think the president cares about the economy. Independents tend to see the economy more negatively than party supporters, especially when the president seems focused on other issues. This pattern has happened with both Biden and Trump administrations, showing that politics affects economic perceptions more than the actual data.

articles.title: Quality Upgrading in Global Supply Chains: Evidence from Colombian Coffee
articles.author: IZA Discussion Papers, Institute of Labor Economics (IZA) | IDEAS/RePEc
articles.summary: This study looks at how improving coffee quality affects Colombian farmers' income. It finds that farmers need guarantees to get paid more for better beans. When buyers enforce rules, farmers earn more, but this is hard under normal market conditions.

articles.title: What is Heather Cox Richardsonism?
articles.author: Nate Silver
articles.summary:
Heather Cox Richardsonism is a faction of Democrats called #Resistance Libs who mix scholarly tone with partisan cheerleading.  
Silver argues they resist self-criticism, lean pro-establishment, and sometimes spread misinformation about politics.  
He thinks their instincts hurt Democratic strategy and that the movement needs scrutiny.

articles.title: News and views
articles.author: David Weakliem (noreply@blogger.com)
articles.summary: New media seems to make people more cynical about politics without pushing them strongly to the left or right. In a survey, those who relied on traditional media were generally more confident in election legitimacy compared to new media users. Overall, new media audiences showed less trust in the political process, regardless of their political leanings.

articles.title: American wokeness
articles.author: David Weakliem (noreply@blogger.com)
articles.summary: People’s views on whether Black people have equal job chances changed a lot since 2015, with more seeing inequality. This shift was linked to media coverage and a gap between American values and reality about racial fairness. However, without strong leaders, these views did not lead to big policy changes to reduce racial inequality.

articles.title: The model underlying R-hat and a Bayesian estimator
articles.author: Bob Carpenter
articles.summary: Bob Carpenter explains a Bayesian model for estimating R-hat, a metric that checks if multiple Markov chains have converged in sampling. The model uses a hierarchical approach to capture variation within and between chains and calculates a probability that R-hat is below a cutoff. He tests the method with simulated data and finds it matches traditional R-hat estimates while providing a full Bayesian uncertainty measure.

articles.title: Documentos de Trabajo. 2602. Non-linear effects of monetary policy shocks on housing: Evidence from a CESEE country
articles.author: Últimas publicaciones - Publicaciones - Banco de España

The results suggest that factors like fixed-rate mortgages and policy limits reduce the impact of monetary policy on household credit.

articles.title: The Pledge
articles.author: Scott Alexander
articles.summary: Charities ask for donations all the time and that can make giving feel confusing and guilt-inducing. The author recommends making a simple, binding pledge (like Giving What We Can) to donate a fixed percent of your income so you give reliably and effectively. If you care about making the world better, decide on a cause, take a pledge, and follow through.

articles.title: Dictatorship of The Donald?

The author suggests that traditional media helps provide clarity in interpreting news, unlike new media, which can cause confusion.

articles.title: For they/them

Both parties now see serving wealthy people less favorably, reflecting a rise in anti-elitist feelings.

articles.author: IZA Discussion Papers, Institute of Labor Economics (IZA) | IDEAS/RePEc

articles.title: How I use LLMs

articles.title: Education, partisanship, and the world

articles.title: From Treatment to Safety: The Role of Substance Use Treatment in Preventing Intimate Partner Violence -- by Mevlude Akbulut-Yuksel, Dhaval M. Dave, Bilge Erten, Pinar Keskin, Catarina R. Meneses

articles.title: Totally unfair

articles.title: Racial and Gendered Impacts of International Students on Domestic Peers

articles.title: Studying sex ratios is just a lot harder than you think:  effects are tiny and variation is large.
articles.author: Andrew
articles.summary: Studying sex ratios at birth is very difficult because the effects are tiny and hard to detect. Many studies find big results, but these are often just random noise or bias. Real differences exist but are very small, needing huge samples to study reliably.

articles.title: An idea for getting approximately calibrated 50% subjective probability ranges
articles.author: Andrew
articles.summary: People often make their 50% probability intervals too narrow, so the true value falls outside more than half the time. Andrew suggests starting with your natural guess and then widening the interval by about 1.5 times on a log scale to get a better 50% range. This method helps adjust for common underestimation and gives more reliable probability intervals.
